evals.name: rolling-update-readiness-probe-missing
evals.prompt: My deployment has 3 replicas and I'm doing a rolling update. The new pods keep getting traffic before they're actually ready — users are hitting 502 errors during rollouts. I have a liveness probe on /healthz but nothing else. What's wrong and how do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: The missing readinessProbe is the root cause: without it, Kubernetes adds pods to Service endpoints as soon as they start, regardless of whether they can serve traffic. Add a readinessProbe with failureThreshold: 1 and periodSeconds: 15 so pods are removed from endpoints immediately on failure. Also set maxUnavailable: 0 and maxSurge: 25% so the rollout adds capacity before removing old pods, preventing any capacity drop during the transition.
expectations: Identifies the missing readinessProbe (not liveness alone) as the direct cause of traffic routing to unready pods
Recommends readinessProbe with failureThreshold: 1 to remove pods from endpoints immediately on failure, not a higher threshold
Recommends maxUnavailable: 0 (not maxUnavailable: 1) with the explicit reasoning that 1 drops capacity to 2/3 and risks downtime if another pod fails simultaneously
Sets livenessProbe.failureThreshold higher than readinessProbe.failureThreshold so the pod gets a recovery window before restart
Avoids recommending livenessProbe alone as a substitute for readinessProbe

evals.name: node-drain-safe-reboot
evals.prompt: I need to reboot a worker node for kernel updates. It's running several production pods. What's the correct procedure to do this safely, and what should I watch out for on a single-node cluster?
evals.expected_output: Drain the node with kubectl drain <node> --delete-emptydir-data=true --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=360s before rebooting. This evicts all pods and marks the node unschedulable. After maintenance, run kubectl uncordon <node> to re-enable scheduling. On a single-node cluster, draining evicts CoreDNS, causing a brief cluster-wide DNS outage — plan accordingly.
expectations: Provides the exact drain flags --delete-emptydir-data=true --ignore-daemonsets --timeout=360s, not a bare kubectl drain
Explains that --ignore-daemonsets is required because DaemonSet pods cannot be moved and would otherwise block the drain
Includes kubectl uncordon <node> as the post-maintenance step to re-enable scheduling
Specifically warns that draining a single-node cluster evicts CoreDNS and causes a DNS outage
Avoids recommending kubectl cordon alone (without drain) as sufficient preparation before a reboot

evals.name: cicd-patch-resourceversion-conflict
evals.prompt: I'm writing a CI/CD deployment script that fetches the live Deployment YAML, patches the image tag, and applies it back with kubectl apply. My script works fine normally but fails intermittently with a conflict error when the HPA or ArgoCD is also active. How should I handle this?
evals.expected_output: The conflict is caused by keeping resourceVersion in the patched YAML. By default, deleting resourceVersion makes the apply unconditional, which works for single-writer pipelines but causes a reconciliation fight with GitOps tools or HPA. The correct fix for multi-writer environments is to keep resourceVersion to detect concurrent modifications, then retry the read-patch-apply loop on conflict. Also look up containers and env vars by name, never by index, since order can change.
expectations: Identifies resourceVersion as the optimistic concurrency token causing the conflict, not a generic 'race condition'
Distinguishes the two valid approaches: delete resourceVersion for single-writer CI vs. keep it and retry the read-patch-apply loop when GitOps or HPA is active
Explicitly names ArgoCD and HPA as the multi-writer scenario that makes unconditional apply dangerous (reconciliation fight)
Recommends looking up containers and env vars by name (not by index) because order in the containers array can change
Avoids recommending kubectl set image as a universal fix, since it does not update annotations like kubernetes.io/change-cause or env vars used for version tracing
